title: Testing & Assurance
subtitle: How you actually know controls work

hook.metaphor: Believing your smoke alarms work isn't the same as knowing. Once a year you press the button on each one and listen for the beep. Control assurance is that button-press at organisational scale: deliberately testing controls so you can *prove*, not just hope, that they fire when needed.
hook.scenario: An executive tells the board, 'our controls are strong.' A director asks the killer question: 'How do you know?' The honest answer is either 'we tested a sample of them last quarter and here are the results' — or an awkward silence. Which firm would you trust?

learn: Assurance turns belief into evidence through **testing**. You can't check every transaction, so you take a **sample** and inspect it: were the approvals there? did the reconciliation actually happen? does the evidence exist? Sampling lets you draw a reliable conclusion about the whole from a manageable few.

When a test fails, you've found a **control deficiency** — and the severity has a ladder (especially in financial-reporting / **SOX**-style regimes): a minor **deficiency**, a more serious **significant deficiency**, and the worst, a **material weakness** (a flaw serious enough that a meaningful error could slip through undetected). Naming the severity drives how urgently it's fixed and whether it must be disclosed.

Assurance comes in layers — the **three lines of defence** again. The business checks its own controls (1st line); risk/compliance monitors and tests (2nd line); and **internal audit** independently and objectively tests the lot (3rd line). On top, **external auditors** give an independent opinion to the outside world. Independence rises as you go up — because marking your own homework isn't assurance.

The point of all this isn't to generate findings for their own sake; it's to give leaders and regulators **justified confidence**. 'Our controls are strong, and here's the testing that shows it' is a fundamentally different — and far more defensible — statement than 'trust us.'
bridge: So at work, you can cut through any 'our controls are fine' claim with the director's question: **'How do you know — what was tested, by whom, and what did it find?'** Real assurance has evidence behind it: samples tested, deficiencies rated and tracked, and independent eyes involved. No evidence means it's a hope, not an assurance.

interactions.title: Assurance vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Testing a subset to conclude about the whole
A control that failed a test or isn't working
A flaw serious enough that a big error could go undetected
Independent in-house testing of all controls (3rd line)
An outside party giving an independent opinion

interactions.title: 'How do you know?'
interactions.prompt: The board asks how the executive knows controls are strong. The trustworthy answer is…

options.label: 'I've been here 20 years — trust me.'
options.feedback: Experience and confidence aren't evidence. 'Trust me' is exactly the answer that precedes a nasty surprise. Assurance needs testing behind it.

options.label: 'We tested a risk-based sample; here are the results and the fixes underway.'
options.feedback: Exactly. That's justified confidence: evidence from testing, deficiencies rated and tracked, remediation in flight. It's defensible to the board and the regulator.

options.label: 'Our policies are very detailed.'
options.feedback: Detailed policies prove design intent, not that controls operate. The question was 'how do you *know* they work?' — that needs testing, not page count.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Assurance is pressing the smoke-alarm button at scale — sampling and testing controls so you can prove they work, rating any deficiencies, with independent audit on top — turning 'trust us' into 'here's the evidence.'”

options: You can't test everything, so a sample lets you reliably conclude about the whole
It's required to test only one item
checkpoint.explain: Testing a representative subset draws a sound conclusion about the full population efficiently.

options: A flaw serious enough that a meaningful error could go undetected
checkpoint.explain: It's the most severe rung of control deficiency — serious enough to risk a material undetected error, often disclosable.

checkpoint.q: Internal audit's role in assurance is to…
options: Independently and objectively test that controls work (the 3rd line)
checkpoint.explain: As the third line, internal audit provides independent assurance — you can't credibly mark your own homework.
